text: Welcome back to the show. Today: the famous wallet experiment. Researchers left thousands of wallets in cities around the world — some empty, some full of cash — and waited. The surprising outcome? Most people returned them. And here is the twist: wallets WITH money were returned MORE often than empty ones. Why? Researchers say people were not afraid of consequences — they simply did not want to see themselves as thieves. They knew they would regret it. So, before our debate: what would YOU do?

My card was: if you could read minds, would you use the power? Honestly? I would not. I know it sounds crazy — it would be so tempting! But think about the consequences: if I knew everything my friends really thought, I would lose my real friendships. Some things should stay private. So if I had that power, I would turn it off. That is my principle, and I would not hesitate.
Dilemma
Consequence
Outcome
Scenario
Hesitate
Tempting
Principle
Regret

Sofia presents the wallet scenario. She asks: what would you do? Mark answers: he would return it, despite the temptation. Sofia attacks with a harder scenario: the best friend. Mark gives his honest final answer.
What would you do if...?
If I found it, I would return it.
If I were you, I would not hesitate.
It would be tempting, but it is against my principles.
Every choice has consequences.
If I found a wallet, I would return it.
What would you do if you saw a friend cheating?
If I were you, I would tell the teacher.
Keeping the money would be tempting.
I would not hesitate for a second.
What would you do if you won a million dollars?
My perspective was changed by that experience.
Apps are designed to keep us engaged.
I would not hesitate if the outcome were clear.
Okay, card seven: if you found a wallet with five hundred dollars, what would you do? Be honest!
Honestly? Keeping it would be tempting. But I would take it to the school office.
Why? Nobody would know. There would be zero consequences.
I would know. That is the consequence. If I kept it, I would regret it every time I used that money.
Interesting. So it is a question of principle, not fear. New scenario: what if your best friend found it?
Ouch. That is a real dilemma. I would not report him... but I would try to convince him to return it.
And if he refused? Would you hesitate then?
Yes, I would. And that is the honest answer — the outcome would depend on the friendship. Your turn, Sofia!
